lesson: Push all items onto a plain list with heapq.heappush then heapq.heappop repeatedly for a min-ordering; use heapq.nsmallest(n, iterable) when you only need the top n without draining the heap.
Find the kth largest by keeping a size-k min-heap: push each element, pop when len(heap)>k; heap[0] is the kth largest in O(n log k) without sorting.

When merging k sorted lists manually, push tuples (value, source_index, elem_index) not just value, so equal-priority ties break on source_index before ever comparing non-orderable payloads and raising TypeError.
task: merge k sorted lists with tie-breaking tuples

lesson: Maintain a running median with a negated max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half; after every push, cross-balance one element between them so sizes differ by at most 1 and the median is O(log n) per insert.

For cooldown-constrained scheduling (e.g. task scheduler), pop the most-frequent task off a max-heap (negated counts) each tick and stash it in a deque keyed by its next-available tick, re-pushing to the heap only when that tick arrives.

heapq has no decrease-key: implement update/remove by keeping an entry_finder dict from item to its [priority, count, item] list, mutating the item slot to a REMOVED sentinel and skipping sentinel entries lazily on pop.

Simulate decrease-key in Dijkstra by pushing a new (dist, node) tuple whenever you find a shorter path instead of mutating the heap, and skip a popped node if it's already in the visited set — the stale, larger-distance duplicate is discarded for free.
task: Dijkstra shortest path with heapq lazy deletion

lesson: For sliding-window median, don't rebuild heaps on eviction: tombstone the outgoing value in a delayed-removal counter dict, adjust logical small/large sizes immediately, and only pop the tombstoned value from a heap once it actually surfaces at heap[0].
task: sliding window median with two heaps and lazy deletion
